content.text: Containers are lightweight, portable execution environments that package an application together with its runtime dependencies, configuration, and filesystem — yet share the host operating system kernel rather than emulating a full machine. Docker is the toolchain that builds, distributes, and runs these containers at a level of usability that made containerization mainstream. For agentic AI systems — which rely on precise Python versions, native libraries, system-level tooling, and complex multi-service orchestration — Docker closes the gap between a working local environment and a reproducible production deployment.
Docker is an open-source platform composed of a build tool, a runtime, and an image distribution system. At its centre is the

), a long-running background service that manages the entire container lifecycle: building images from instructions, pulling and pushing them to registries, creating container processes, and managing their networking and storage. A thin
) communicates with the daemon over a REST API.
— an immutable, layered snapshot of a filesystem. A

is a running (or stopped) process spawned from that image, given its own isolated view of the filesystem, network, and process table via Linux kernel primitives. Because images are immutable and portable, the same image SHA produces identical behaviour on a developer laptop, a CI runner, and a Kubernetes node running in any cloud region.
Docker Compose extends this model to multi-container applications by letting you declare an entire service graph — agent API, task queue, vector store, database — in a single
file and start it with one command. For local development and integration testing of agentic systems, Compose is often more immediately useful than Kubernetes.

Agentic AI workloads amplify the classic dependency-hell problem. An orchestrator written for Python 3.12, tiktoken 0.7, and a patched version of httpx will silently behave differently if a teammate's machine has Python 3.11 or a system-level library mismatch. Multiply that across CI, staging, and GPU inference nodes and the surface area for environment drift becomes enormous. Docker eliminates this by making the runtime specification part of the codebase: a
is a machine-executable description of every layer from the base OS image to the final application process.

Beyond reproducibility, Docker matters for agentic deployments because container images are the unit of atomic deployment. Rollbacks become as simple as re-pointing the service to the previous image tag. Blue-green deploys, canary releases, and zero-downtime restarts all operate at the image level. When a Celery worker crashes under high token-generation load, the container orchestrator restarts a fresh instance from the same image in under a second — no cleanup, no stale state, no partial re-installation of dependencies.

For eval pipelines and batch inference jobs, Docker ensures that the model loading code, tokenizer config, and sampling parameters used in a CI eval job are byte-for-byte identical to what runs in production — which is the precondition for trusting evaluation results.
— same image digest across developer laptops, CI, staging, and production means no more 'works on my machine' failures.
— containers get their own filesystem, process namespace, and network interface. An agent worker process cannot interfere with another service's dependencies or file system.
— an image built on an x86 Linux machine runs on any Linux host (and, with emulation, on ARM/Apple Silicon). Migrate between cloud providers without touching application code.
— containers start in milliseconds to seconds rather than the minutes a VM boot requires. Horizontal scaling of agent worker pools can respond to queue depth in real time.
— deploying a specific image tag is atomic and reversible. If a new worker image introduces a regression, revert to the previous tag with a single command.
— unchanged layers are reused across builds. A pip-install layer that takes 90 s on first build is a cache hit (< 1 s) when only application source changes.
— spin up a complete agent system (API, Redis, Postgres, a mock LLM endpoint) with one command and tear it down just as cleanly.
— containers share the host kernel. A kernel exploit can escape container isolation. VMs provide a stronger security boundary when running untrusted third-party agent code.
— a careless Dockerfile pulling in build tools and debug utilities can produce images exceeding 2 GB, slowing down registry push and ECS task launch.
— container filesystems are ephemeral. Databases, vector stores, and model caches must be pinned to named volumes or external services to survive container restarts.

— bridge networks, host networking, overlay networks, and published ports each have different characteristics. Getting service-to-service communication right in Compose and Kubernetes takes deliberate configuration.
— secrets accidentally copied into an image layer are preserved in the layer cache even after a subsequent RUN rm command, because each layer is immutable.
— images built for linux/amd64 do not natively run on Apple Silicon (linux/arm64). Multi-platform builds with
are necessary for teams with mixed hardware.
Each Docker image is a stack of read-only layers managed by a storage driver (typically

instruction in a Dockerfile produces one new layer containing only the files that changed. Layers are content-addressed: their identifier is the SHA-256 of the layer's content. When Docker builds an image, it checks whether a layer for the same instruction and parent-layer hash already exists in the local cache. If so, it reuses that layer — the cache hit. This is why instruction order in a Dockerfile matters so much for build speed: place the instructions whose inputs change least frequently (e.g., installing OS packages and Python dependencies) before instructions whose inputs change often (e.g., copying application source).

When a container starts, Docker adds a thin writable layer (the 'container layer') on top of the read-only image layers. All writes by the running process — log files, temporary state, downloaded assets — go to this writable layer via copy-on-write. When the container is removed, its writable layer is discarded. The underlying image layers are untouched and can be shared by multiple concurrent containers running from the same image, saving disk space.
Container isolation is not a Docker invention — it is a composition of Linux kernel features that have existed for many years:

— each container gets its own view of the process table (PID namespace), network interfaces (net namespace), filesystem mounts (mnt namespace), hostname (UTS namespace), and IPC objects. Processes in one container cannot see or signal processes in another.
— impose per-container resource limits. A Celery worker container can be capped at 2 CPU cores and 4 GB of RAM so it cannot starve the API container on the same host.
— system-call filtering profiles restrict which kernel calls a containerized process may make, reducing the attack surface even if the process is compromised.
— the union filesystem implementation that merges read-only image layers with the writable container layer, presenting a single unified directory tree to the process.

, executes each instruction sequentially in a temporary intermediate container, and commits the resulting filesystem snapshot as a new layer. The process is deterministic given the same inputs (base image, Dockerfile content, and build context files). The final layer is tagged with a human-readable name and version (e.g.,
) while also being addressable by its content hash (

Pushing an image to a registry uploads only the layers that the registry does not already have (delta push). Pulling an image downloads only the layers absent from the local cache. This content-addressable, layer-deduplication model makes Docker registries efficient even when many images share common base layers.

attrs.alt: Docker architecture diagram showing CLI, Daemon, Registry, Image Store, running Containers, and Volumes

content.text: The diagram shows the main runtime components: the Docker CLI sends commands to the Docker daemon over a local Unix socket. The daemon manages an image store (overlay2 layer cache on disk), creates container processes using Linux namespaces and cgroups, and communicates with registries to push or pull image layers. Each running container shares the kernel but has its own network interface and filesystem view. Named volumes are managed outside the container lifecycle and can be mounted into one or more containers for persistent storage.
For agentic systems, a typical compose stack might run four containers: an
pulling tasks from a Redis queue, a
service. All four share a compose-managed network and can reach each other by service name. The developer's workflow is a single
and the entire stack is available at localhost ports.
Use Docker in the following situations:
Deploying any service to a cloud environment
— ECS, Cloud Run, Fly.io, and Render all consume Docker images. Building an image is not optional for these platforms.
— Celery and RQ workers benefit from the same image as the API so they share the exact same agent code and dependency versions.
— Compose eliminates the need for every developer to manually install and configure Postgres, Redis, and other infrastructure services.
— containerised eval scripts ensure the Python environment used in evaluation matches the one used in production inference.
; the full stack starts without any manual environment setup.
Consider skipping Docker (or deferring it) in these cases:
— a one-off script run in a virtual environment is fine; the overhead of a Dockerfile is not justified.
— if the target platform (AWS Lambda, Vercel Edge) packages your code in its own way, a custom Dockerfile may conflict with the platform's build system.
GPU workloads requiring specific CUDA configuration

— Docker supports GPU passthrough via the NVIDIA Container Toolkit, but the host driver version constrains the CUDA version inside the container. Validate this early; it is a frequent source of frustration.
The key principle is to order instructions from least-changing to most-changing so that the expensive dependency-installation layer is a cache hit on every code-only change.

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM python:3.12-slim AS base

# System-level packages that rarely change (one RUN keeps layers minimal)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    curl ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /app

# --- dependency layer (cached unless requirements change) ---
COPY requirements.lock .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.lock

# --- application source (invalidates on every code change) ---
COPY src/ src/

# Non-root user for production security
RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" appuser
USER appuser

# Health check so orchestrators know when the container is ready
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=10s \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "agent_platform.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
file, Docker sends the entire build context (including
, test fixtures, and compiled artefacts) to the daemon on every build. This slows context transfer and can accidentally copy sensitive files into the image.
Step 3 — Docker Compose for local development

# compose.yaml
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file: .env
    depends_on:
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    volumes:
      - ./src:/app/src   # hot-reload during development

  worker:
    build: .
    command: celery -A agent_platform.tasks worker --loglevel=info
    env_file: .env
    depends_on:
      - redis

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: agentdb
      POSTGRES_USER: agent
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U agent"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

volumes:
  pgdata:

# Build image locally and tag
docker build -t agent-api:local .

# Run single container with env file
docker run --rm -p 8000:8000 --env-file .env agent-api:local

# Start full compose stack detached
docker compose up -d

# Tail logs from the API service
docker compose logs -f api

# Inspect running containers
docker ps

# Stop and remove containers (preserves named volumes)
docker compose down
A stripped-down Dockerfile for a FastAPI-based agent endpoint. Shows the minimal moving parts before adding health checks and multi-stage builds.

FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY main.py .
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
Example 2 — Multi-stage build for a smaller production image
Multi-stage builds let a heavy builder image compile or install packages, then copy only the final artefacts into a lean runtime image. This is especially useful for packages with C extensions (e.g.,
) that require build tools at compile time but not at runtime.

# ---- builder stage ----
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /build
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gcc
COPY requirements.lock .
# Install into an isolated prefix to copy cleanly
RUN pip install --prefix=/install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.lock

# ---- runtime stage ----
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
# Copy only the installed packages, not the compiler
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY src/ src/
RUN adduser --disabled-password appuser
USER appuser
CMD ["uvicorn", "agent_platform.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
The runtime image produced by this build typically contains only the Python interpreter, installed packages, and application source — no
caches. Final image size drops from ~800 MB to ~200 MB for a typical agent API.
A production agentic system at moderate scale (200 req/min peak) commonly uses the following Docker-based deployment pattern on AWS:
A GitHub Actions CI pipeline runs
and pushes the image to ECR tagged with the git commit SHA (e.g.,

An ECS service definition references the immutable SHA tag. Terraform applies the update, triggering a rolling deployment: ECS starts new tasks from the new image before draining connections from old tasks.
Celery worker tasks run in a second ECS service using the same image with a different
override. Workers scale horizontally based on the SQS queue depth alarm.

Postgres and Redis run as managed services (RDS, ElastiCache) outside Docker; the containers connect via environment variables injected from AWS Secrets Manager at task launch — never baked into the image.
An Application Load Balancer terminates HTTPS and forwards to the ECS tasks on port 8000. Container health checks are the gating condition for ALB target registration.
The deployment CI step looks like this:

# In CI (GitHub Actions) — build, push, deploy
IMAGE_TAG=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
ECR_URI=123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/agent-api

aws ecr get-login-password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin $ECR_URI

docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t $ECR_URI:$IMAGE_TAG .
docker push $ECR_URI:$IMAGE_TAG

# Update ECS service to use new image
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster prod \
  --service agent-api \
  --force-new-deployment \
  --task-definition "$(
    aws ecs describe-task-definition --task-definition agent-api \
      | jq --arg img "$ECR_URI:$IMAGE_TAG" '.taskDefinition | .containerDefinitions[0].image = $img | @json' \
      | xargs aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json | jq -r '.taskDefinition.taskDefinitionArn'
  )"

The default Docker container user is root (UID 0). If a vulnerability in the application allows command execution, the attacker has full root access inside the container — and on any bind-mounted host paths. Always create and switch to a non-root user:

# Wrong — runs as root by default
COPY src/ src/
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app"]

# Right — switch to a non-root user before CMD
RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" appuser
USER appuser
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
instruction that sets a secret env var or writes a credential file leaves that data in the immutable layer even if a subsequent layer deletes it. The layer is accessible via

# Wrong — ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is baked into layer sha256:abc...
ENV ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-abc123
RUN pip install ...

# Right — inject secrets at runtime, never at build time
# (set via --env-file, ECS task secrets, or Secrets Manager)
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
instruction sends the entire project directory — including
files — to the daemon as build context. This breaks cache consistency and risks leaking secrets.
means there is no audit trail of which build is running and rollback requires rebuilding from source. Always push and deploy an immutable tag (git SHA or semantic version):

# Wrong — latest is mutable, rollback is undefined
docker push myregistry/agent-api:latest

# Right — immutable tag for every deploy
docker push myregistry/agent-api:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
Use slim or distroless base images
removes Debian development packages; distroless removes the shell and package manager entirely, reducing attack surface and image size.
Order Dockerfile instructions by change frequency
— OS packages first, pip install second, application source last. This maximises cache reuse in CI.
) make every deploy traceable and rollbacks trivial.
Add a HEALTHCHECK in the Dockerfile
— orchestrators (ECS, Kubernetes) use health checks to gate traffic and detect stuck containers.
— separate builder and runtime images to exclude compilers, headers, and build-time tooling from the deployed artifact.

— run the API server and the Celery worker as separate containers from the same image with a different command, not as multiple processes in one container. This allows independent scaling and crash isolation.
Set explicit resource limits in production
— cgroup limits prevent a runaway LLM loop from consuming all host memory and starving other services.
Pin base image digests in production
prevents silent base-image drift between builds.

Build time is dominated by the pip install layer. With correct instruction ordering, a typical agent API Dockerfile achieves cache hits on the pip layer for 95%+ of CI runs (only dependency-lock-file changes trigger a reinstall). The difference between a cache hit and cache miss build is often 2 minutes versus 8–12 minutes. Measure with:

# See which layers hit cache vs. rebuilt
docker build --progress=plain -t agent-api:local . 2>&1 | grep -E "CACHED|RUN"
Large images slow registry pushes, ECS task launches, and Kubernetes pod scheduling. Use
to inspect layer contents and find wasted space:

# Install dive (https://github.com/wagoodman/dive)
dive agent-api:local

# Quick size audit without dive
docker image history agent-api:local --format "{{.Size}}\t{{.CreatedBy}}" | sort -rh | head -10

Typical size targets for agent API images: under 300 MB for slim Python images, under 150 MB for distroless. GPU inference images carrying CUDA libraries are unavoidably larger (3–8 GB) — use multi-stage builds to ensure application code changes do not trigger a full CUDA layer re-download.

LLM orchestration loops can accumulate unbounded memory if context windows are not managed. Set container resource limits so a stuck agent process is killed and restarted rather than OOM-killing the host:

# compose.yaml — resource limits
services:
  worker:
    image: agent-api:local
    command: celery -A agent_platform.tasks worker
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: "2.0"
          memory: 4G
        reservations:
          memory: 1G
— GUI for macOS and Windows that bundles the daemon, BuildKit, Compose, and a Kubernetes single-node cluster. Useful for local development on non-Linux machines.
— an extended build driver supporting multi-platform image builds (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64 in a single push), inline build caching, and secret mounts at build time.
— the standard tool for defining and running multi-container local stacks. Supported natively by
(v2, the plugin) and by most CI providers.
— a terminal UI for exploring image layers and finding space wasted by unnecessary files included in layers.
— a fast, open-source vulnerability scanner for container images. Scans OS packages and Python dependencies against CVE databases.
— a Dockerfile linter that enforces best practices and catches common mistakes at authoring time.
— the default build backend since Docker 23.0. Provides parallel layer building, secrets mounts (
), SSH agent forwarding, and improved cache semantics.
Using BuildKit's secret mount to access private package indexes at build time without baking tokens into layers:

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.lock .
# --mount=type=secret exposes the secret only during this RUN instruction
# It is NEVER written to any image layer
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=pip_token \
    PIP_INDEX_URL=$(cat /run/secrets/pip_token) \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.lock
COPY src/ src/
CMD ["uvicorn", "agent_platform.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

# Pass the secret at build time — not stored in the image
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
  --secret id=pip_token,env=PRIVATE_PYPI_URL \
  -t agent-api:local .

Podman is a drop-in CLI replacement for Docker that runs containers without a central daemon process (daemonless) and supports fully rootless operation. For teams on RHEL/Fedora or in environments where running a privileged daemon is a security concern, Podman is often preferred. Its OCI compliance means Docker images run unchanged.
Container images inherit vulnerabilities from their base image and from every installed package. Scan images in CI before pushing to a registry:

# Scan with Trivy (https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy)
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL agent-api:local

# Fail CI if critical CVEs are found
trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL agent-api:local
Inject secrets at container launch time from a secrets manager rather than from environment variables in compose files (which may be committed to source control):
Running as a non-root user combined with a read-only root filesystem limits the damage an exploited container process can do:

docker run --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp \          # allow writes only to /tmp
  --user 1000:1000 \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  agent-api:local
Define a health check in the Dockerfile so orchestrators can distinguish a crashed container from a healthy one. In Compose, add a restart policy so crashed workers recover automatically:

services:
  worker:
    image: agent-api:local
    restart: on-failure:5   # restart on crash, give up after 5 attempts
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "celery -A agent_platform.tasks inspect ping || exit 1"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 30s
Drop all Linux capabilities except the minimum required and prevent privilege escalation to defend against container escape:

docker run \
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE \   # only if binding port < 1024
  --security-opt=no-new-privileges \
  agent-api:local
A Docker image is a stack of immutable, content-addressed filesystem layers; a container is a running process given an isolated view of those layers plus a thin writable layer on top.
Order Dockerfile instructions from least-changing (OS packages, pip install) to most-changing (application source) to maximise layer cache hits and keep CI builds fast.
Never bake secrets into image layers — inject them at runtime via environment variables, secret mounts, or cloud secrets managers; the build-time layer cache preserves secrets permanently.

Docker Compose provides a production-faithful local environment for multi-service agentic stacks (API, worker, Redis, Postgres) with a single command; it eliminates manual infrastructure setup for every developer.
Run containers as a non-root user, set CPU and memory cgroup limits, define a HEALTHCHECK, and use immutable SHA image tags in production deploys.
Multi-stage builds reduce final image size significantly by separating compiler toolchains from the runtime image; smaller images deploy faster and have a smaller vulnerability surface.

Container isolation relies on Linux namespaces and cgroups, not full virtualisation; the shared kernel is a security trade-off that Podman's rootless mode and additional seccomp/AppArmor profiles can partially mitigate.
